text: One overcast morning in November, a visitor named Greta arrived in our small village to clothe her great aspiration in real fieldwork. She had no jeep, only a battered lorry and a mobile phone, yet her endeavour to study the local environment was substantial. Whenever she could, she would crouch beside a mossy bench, watching a swift dart past the corner of the lawn, while moss spread over an old estate wall. With discretion she would not allow the oncoming madness of tourist crowds to disturb the place she came to adore. Her future, she said, depended on this present procedure of patient observation, and she kept a varied encyclopedia of notes. Her behavior was orderly: she would blink at the sun, freshen her mind with cold water, and own up to any uncertain finding. A wrinkled clause in her grant required a chairperson to retrieve every diagram and piece of information she produced, so she sent each gift of data ahead via a tiny projector. The hump of a distant hill, the violet sky, a thorn-edged path past a frock-clad scarecrow, the buckle on her bag, all earned a compliment in her diary. She would mutter no allegation of injustice when funding was uncertain; instead she would devour her homework, fiddle with a tool, and present a calm aspect even when antagonism flared. Her old mistress in alchemy had taught her that experimentation, not rapture or mere manipulation of numbers, brings discovery. The villagers spoke a soft dialect that she found beautiful, and she never grew unduly impatient with it. Once, a puncture in the lorry tyre and an antagonist's dishonorable trick delayed her, but she did not buy excuses. She would charge ahead, more valid than ever, slot her tickets and visa into a drawer, and crouch again. A cashier in the next village, recovering from tuberculosis, would adore her delicious bread and edible berries, sharing kin-like warmth. She studied a digit on her ration card, a partition between fields, the aerial above a fire-engine, and even the embroidery on a buckle. Each clause of her novel report, though it left her unconscious of fame, recorded the plague years, the crazy delight of an ad-free dawn, and the buckle of frost. Upside-down in the grass, she would take a slow breath, thank the morning, and feel her crucial discovery blossom.
translation: 十一月一个阴沉的早晨，一位名叫格蕾塔的访客来到我们的小村庄，要把她伟大的抱负付诸真正的田野工作。她没有吉普车，只有一辆破旧的卡车和一部手机，但她研究当地环境的努力是相当可观的。只要有可能，她就会蹲在长满苔藓的长椅旁，看着一只雨燕掠过草坪的角落，而苔藓爬满了一堵旧庄园的墙。出于谨慎，她不愿让蜂拥而至的游客的疯狂打扰她前来钟爱的这个地方。她说，她的未来取决于眼下这套耐心观察的程序，她记下了内容多样、像百科全书般的笔记。她的举止井然有序：她会对着太阳眨眼，用冷水让头脑清醒，并坦承任何不确定的发现。她经费里一条带皱褶的条款要求一位主席收回她产出的每一张图表和每一份信息，于是她通过一台小投影仪把每份数据礼物送往前方。远处山丘的隆起、紫罗兰色的天空、一条被荆棘环绕、经过身着旧外套的稻草人的小路、她包上的扣环，都在她的日记里得到一句赞美。当经费不确定时，她不会嘟囔任何关于不公的指控；相反，她会埋头做功课，摆弄工具，即使敌意爆发也保持镇定的神情。她从前教她炼金术的女主人曾教导她，带来发现的是实验，而非狂喜或仅仅对数字的操纵。村民们说着一种她觉得很美的柔和方言，她从不会对此过分地不耐烦。有一次，卡车轮胎被扎破，加上一个对手不光彩的诡计耽误了她，但她不买账找借口。她会奋勇向前，比以往更加坚定有效，把车票和签证塞进抽屉，再次蹲下身。隔壁村一位刚从肺结核中康复的收银员会喜爱她美味的面包和可食用的浆果，分享如亲人般的温暖。她研究着配给卡上的一个数字、田地间的隔断、消防车上方的天线，甚至扣环上的刺绣。她那部小说般报告的每一条款，尽管让她对名声毫无知觉，却记录了瘟疫的岁月、无广告黎明的疯狂喜悦，以及霜冻的扣合。她倒立在草丛中，深深呼吸，感谢这个清晨，感到她那至关重要的发现正在绽放。
